senses.gloss: the science of grammar as a textual discipline and body of rules
senses.justification: Verses 1.11-15 refer to grammar as a science (vidyā), a discipline, a meta-discipline grounded in textual analysis, and a gateway to the sciences.

senses.gloss: the transformative power or faculty enabling direct access to the absolute
senses.justification: Verses 1.12 and 1.18-22 present grammar as a power (śakti) and method (yoga) that provides the direct path and enables realization of Brahman.

senses.gloss: the imperishable, eternal reality; Brahman itself
senses.justification: Verse 1.9 identifies akṣara as the eternally imperishable that IS purification itself, the syllable through which supreme knowledge comes.

senses.gloss: phonetic signs, syllables, or phonetic elements; sound-letters
senses.justification: Verses 1.18-22 refer to aksara-smrti's signs as the phonetic apparatus—the differentiated sounds (varnas) housed within the highest speech.

senses.gloss: the ultimate principle of righteousness, virtue, and merit; the ethical-ontological ground
senses.justification: Verses 1.24-26, 1.27, 1.29 present dharma as the final aim of grammar, the principle whose realization grammar enables and whose means grammar provides.

senses.gloss: the empirical, transmitted paths and practices of virtue; established customs and traditions
senses.justification: Verses 1.30-31 describe dharma as the unbroken paths established through tradition and world-practice, grounded in empirical continuity rather than reasoning alone.

senses.gloss: action as the ultimate principle; the basis of cosmic order and ritual efficacy
senses.justification: Verse 1.6 references karma as the absolute ground underlying the manifestation of ritual power and the cosmic order.

senses.gloss: the causal power or force of past actions; karma as the generative power producing attainments
senses.justification: Verse 1.36 states that the siddhis (attainments) of ancestors, raksasas, and pisacas are born of karma—karma as the power that generates supernatural powers.

senses.gloss: semantic power; the force by which words signify and mean
senses.justification: Verse 1.23 discusses the eternal relation between word and meaning, treating artha as the inherent power (śakti) of words to convey significance.

senses.gloss: meaning as a property conveyed or as the attribute of meaningfulness
senses.justification: Verse 1.27 establishes that both correct and incorrect words succeed equally in artha-pratyayana (conveying meaning), treating artha as a property both possess.

senses.gloss: purpose, aim, or ultimate goal; that for which all activity is undertaken
senses.justification: Verse 1.29 invokes purpose (artha): no one establishes a purposeless system, so grammar's codification must serve an ultimate artha (here identified with dharma).

senses.gloss: a matter, subject, or object of knowledge; the thing being discussed or inferred
senses.justification: Verse 1.34 refers to a matter inferred with great effort, treating artha as the subject-matter of inference that remains vulnerable to reversal.

senses.gloss: ultimate knowledge of purification and realization
senses.justification: Verse 1.9 identifies vidyā as the true purification itself, the ultimate knowledge reached through the syllable Om that contradicts no doctrine.

senses.gloss: the empirical sciences and disciplined bodies of knowledge
senses.justification: Verse 1.15 refers to 'the sciences' (vidyā) as empirical fields of knowledge of which grammar is the final resort and culmination.

senses.gloss: inference as a means of knowledge; anumāna as a pramāṇa or instrument
senses.justification: Verse 1.36 lists anumāna alongside pratyakṣa as one of the two conventional pramāṇas (means of knowledge) whose reach the siddhis transcend.

senses.gloss: inference as an epistemic faculty or cognitive process
senses.justification: Verses 1.32, 1.35, 1.37 discuss anumāna as an epistemic capacity—establishing things by inference, the claim that anumāna exhausts knowledge, and what the highest knowledge transcends.

senses.gloss: perception as a means of knowledge; pratyakṣa as a pramāṇa or instrument
senses.justification: Verse 1.36 lists pratyakṣa alongside anumāna as one of the two conventional pramāṇas (means of knowledge) whose reach the siddhis transcend.

senses.gloss: perception as an epistemic faculty or immediate knowledge
senses.justification: Verse 1.37 describes the highest knowledge as having perception's immediacy (aparokṣa), not distinguishable from perception—pratyakṣa as the character of direct epistemic awareness.
